calendar.matters:
MOTION TO SET ASIDE DEFAULT/DEFAULT JUDGMENT/LEAVE TO DEFEND
Off Calendar Jun-05-2026 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: DEFENDANT CHRIS KAPRAT'S MOTION TO SET ASIDE DEFAULT

Appearances via Zoom:
Christopher Kaprat (slotkicks@gmail.com) defendant and moving party appearing in propria persona;
Alec Cierny of the Cierny Firm (415.259.4646) for plaintiff and opposing party.

Argued and the court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: All of the facts and arguments presented by defendant Chris Kaprat in support of his motion to set aside the default judgment entered against him relate to the merits of plaintiff's claims in this case, and to the defenses Mr. Kaprat believes he has to those claims. But the central, critical inquiry in a motion to set aside a default has nothing to do with the relative strengths or weaknesses of the underlying case; rather, it is whether the moving party has made a showing of mistake, inadvertence, surprise, or excusable neglect such that relief from the default should be granted.

Defendant does not dispute that the summons and amended complaint were properly served on him on January 6, 2026. While his papers describe in detail why he thinks the case against him has no merit and should be dismissed, his only explanation for failing to file a response is that he "believed he had no obligation to respond to a lawsuit seeking to hold him personally liable" for an obligation that, in his view, belonged solely to Slot Kicks LLC. A genuine belief by Mr. Kaprat that the case was meritless - even assuming that belief was correct - does not constitute mistake, inadvertence, surprise, or excusable neglect warranting relief from default. Nor does his mistaken belief that he had no obligation to respond to a lawsuit where - in his view - he had no potential liability. (See, Hopkins & Carley v. Gens (2011) 200 Cal.App.4th 1401, 1412 [An honest mistake of law can provide a valid ground for relief "where a problem is complex and debatable," but relief may be properly denied where the record shows only "ignorance of the law coupled with negligence in ascertaining it."]) Defendant offers no facts showing any effort to ascertain the law with respect to his obligations to file an answer or other responsive pleading to the amended complaint.

While the law favors a trial on the merits, and motions under C.C.P. Section 473(b) are to be liberally construed, a party seeking to set aside a default judgment has the burden of presenting a factual basis to support the requested relief. (Hopkins & Carley v. Gens, supra, 200 Cal.App.4th at 1410.) Defendant has failed to do that here.

Defendant's motion to set aside the default judgment under C.C.P. Section 473(d) is similarly unavailing. He has presented no evidence to support his assertion that the judgment is void on its face, or void for lack of service.

Defendant's motion is denied. Proposed order submitted for review. Judge: Richard C. Darwin, Clerk: E. Larnauti, Not Reported (301/RCD)

MOTION TO SET ASIDE DEFAULT/DEFAULT JUDGMENT/LEAVE TO DEFEND
Off Calendar May-08-2026 Continued To Jun-05-26 At 09:00 A.M. In Dept 301
MINI MINUTES: DEFENDANT CHRISTOPHER KAPRAT'S, AN INDIVIDUAL, MOTION TO SET ASIDE DEFAULT/DEFAULT JUDGMENT/LEAVE TO DEFEND.

No appearance was made by any Party, Counsel, or Interested Person. The court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Defendant Chris Kaprat's motion to set aside default is continued for hearing to June 5, 2026. Kaprat shall file an amended declaration attaching his proposed responsive pleading by no later than May 22, 2026. Plaintiff may file a sur-reply by May 29, 2026.

Code of Civil Procedure, Sec. 473, subdivision (b), provides in relevant part that "[t]he court may, upon any terms as may be just, relieve a party or the party's legal representative from a judgment, dismissal, order, or other proceeding taken against the party through the party's mistake, inadvertence, surprise, or excusable neglect. Application for this relief shall be accompanied by a copy of the answer or other pleading proposed to be filed therein, otherwise the application shall not be granted . . . ."

Kaprat has not attached a proposed answer or other pleading, a mandatory requirement of the statute. In the interests of justice, the court continues the motion to permit him to remedy this defect in his moving papers.

Judge: Christine Van Aken. Clerk: Robert Woods. Not Reported. (301/CVA)
